chapters.title: Let everything happen to you, beauty and terror
chapters.text:
Life keeps throwing challenges your way. You try different coping methods — crying or showing no emotion, talking or staying silent. Nothing works entirely, but everything helps a little.

Kate Bowler wrote this during a tough time in her life. She endured the immense physical pain of cancer affecting her entire body, yet she found that her mind was clear for about an hour each morning. During that time, before the pain kicked in, she wrote these meaningful reflections for the day ahead.

We can be full of hope and fear at the same time.

A few years ago, Kate started wishing everyone "a beautiful, terrible day!" The phrase made her laugh and felt honest. After all, life is often a mix of beauty and struggle, and acknowledging both comforted her.

She wrote a collection of short meditations to help you find hope when battling your anxieties. These reflections will remind you that although fear and pain are real, so is your ability to find moments of peace and clarity. How do you accept it all and move forward with grace? Let's dig into your own beautiful, terrible days and the hope within them.

chapters.title: How to find your calm in chaos
chapters.text:
Is anyone else filled with constant worry that they can't quite explain? Come on, raise your hand! First off, you are not alone. Secondly, you might wonder why everyone feels this way — why this generation feels particularly anxious.

From everyday tasks like remembering to buy milk to more pressing concerns like school shootings and global pandemics, it's no wonder you feel overwhelmed.

There are three types of awareness that contribute to this general sense of worry:

• Apocalyptic Awareness: This one stems from recognizing global issues. If you follow the news regularly, you’ll start to notice the fragility of society through environmental issues, racial injustice, and political instability. These concerns might induce anxiety about the future and make you wonder whether your efforts can make a difference.
• Anxious Awareness: Think about how often you experience your anxieties. What do they look like? Perhaps you second-guess decisions or worry about what might go wrong. This constant state of anxiety contributes to a sense of uneasiness and stress daily.
• Painful Awareness: Pain is an inevitable part of life, an aspect of your experiences and struggles. Whether dealing with health issues or personal setbacks, these challenges can make you feel helpless. Philosopher Simone de Beauvoir calls this "facticity" — the aspects of your existence you can't change. Another philosopher, Martin Heidegger, refers to it as the "thrownness" of human life, where you navigate circumstances you didn't choose.

In addition to all this self-induced stress, modern society emphasizes being self-made. There is a notion that anything is possible with enough determination and the right mindset. But life doesn't always work out that way. Despite your best efforts, health issues, relationship troubles, and daily stresses might get in the way.

Allow yourself to rely on others; it can become your biggest strength.

But realizing that everyone is in this together can be empowering. Developing compassion for yourself and others can encourage people to build stronger communities. Whether you shop at farmers' markets, attend a church service, or play on sports teams, let this be your daily reminder that we need each other. It's normal to rely on others; no one can do everything alone.

chapters.title: There is strength in pain and heartbreak
chapters.text:
When life feels out of control, it's okay to admit, "I have lost control. This is happening to me." Give yourself permission to recognize this and ask for help. You don't have to face everything alone.

Pain and tragedy can feel fresh even years later, stacking up like layers of sediment. When you might feel trapped or weighed down, think of these old hurts like exhibits in a museum. Sometimes, it is necessary to revisit these museum rooms and let fresh air in. It can start from a simple act like talking to someone you trust about your pain. Say, "I'm still really hurting," and let yourself feel the grace in that acknowledgment.

When thinking about somebody's absence in your life, read this meditation:
I admit all the ways their presence and absence shaped me. I bless all the moments when I wished things were different. I have the strength to keep going.

Heartbreak is a unique kind of grief, whether from lost love or other deep connections. It feels like a death without closure. As the world keeps turning, social media may give the illusion that others are moving on happily. But know that you can treat heartbreak like grief. Find comfort in small rituals such as wearing black for a while or talking openly about your feelings with a close friend.

Airing out your suffering is better than keeping it bottled up.

Throughout this, keep in mind that living close to pain and suffering does change you, but not necessarily for the worse. When it's hard to remember this, let this small meditation support you:
With everything happening to me right now, I still deserve compassion. I still deserve good things in life. Even now. Especially now.

Endings are everywhere. Some are clear, like funerals, while others are subtle, like realizing something you cherished is over. We often have rituals for happy moments and only a few for heartbreak, yet there is still beauty in the ashes. So, honor these small, everyday funerals. Feel the weight of both your love and loss. Each act of love and every time you rise after being knocked down is a testament to your strength.

chapters.title: Look for the peace you lost
chapters.text:
Have you ever started one thing and immediately switched to another? The number of distractions in your daily life is another reason you might feel out of control. Finding a moment of peace is hard, and that's okay. Instead of striving to be worry-free, think of peace as coming home to yourself. You are your shelter and safe space. Seek to find rest and calm within, even amidst external chaos.

Your constant connection to technology can make finding these moments of peace even more challenging. You might get lost in the flurry of notifications and updates, surrendering your precious attention without realizing its cost. To find calm, you need to create moments of stillness. Try turning off your phone and being quiet for five minutes. Notice the light in the room, the shadows, or anything beautiful around you. Even such small moments of mindfulness can bring a general sense of peace.

When you have these short moments, make the most of them. Take a few minutes to recite this meditation to appreciate your feeling of the present moment next time you need it:
This is the moment. I can feel it. I'm here now, in the in-between of the past and future. Let yourself stop and stay here. Nothing is pulling you forward or back just yet. This moment is the sweet nothing. Savor it and be present.

Yet sometimes, you may feel that life is truly out of control, and peace seems like an impossible luxury. When this happens, the best thing you can do is take a deep breath and acknowledge your feelings. Don't try to deny or fight your reality.

In a society that values non-stop activity, prioritizing rest can feel like an act of rebellion.

But rest is what keeps you alive. You need to slow down and permit yourself to stop. Reconnecting with yourself is nothing to feel guilty about, you deserve peace and calm.

Repeat this short meditation to remind yourself of it:
You know your body needs it — rest. Rest beyond sleep, rest beyond recreation. You aren't designed to carry all the world's problems on your shoulders. You aren't designed to be perfect. You aren't designed to solve and fix everything. You are made to rest.

chapters.title: You deserve the same
chapters.text:
Forgiveness is hard enough. And amid all the pain you experience, sometimes no one shows up to apologize for the pain they've caused. It's normal to feel hurt when people or situations don't acknowledge the harm done. Growing up with a "suck it up" mentality might have made you resilient, but it's also important to recognize when you need support and allow yourself to say, "I'm really sorry it's been so hard."

When there is no one to give you compassion, give it to yourself.

Taking a moment to breathe can also be immensely helpful when you feel suffocated. Try a simple breath prayer: inhale with the thought "Be here" and exhale with "Be here now." Help yourself focus and calm your mind.

There are also moments when you might feel overwhelmed by others' problems. Sometimes, life's circumstances may force you to become a caregiver. If that's the case, remember to balance empathy with self-care. It's easy to feel selfish for wanting to take care of yourself. You might even feel embarrassed about your needs.

But remind yourself that meeting your needs is just part of being human. You might find this little meditation helpful:
Learn to take what you need. Take that time to unwind when you need it. Reach out for a friendly hand when you need it. Allow yourself to take what you need. Your needs are signs of a life growing and thriving.

Rather than a burden, think of your empathy as a gift. Being attuned to others' feelings without fixing or judging them is valuable to building meaningful relationships. Show up with small acts of kindness and compassion for others.

If you judge people, you have no time to love them. ~ Mother Teresa

But remember, your empathy also needs protection. Allow yourself to receive love and support from others, and keep the exchange of giving and receiving flowing.

Did you know? After experiencing significant vision loss, New York Times columnist Frank Bruni found inspiration in the struggles of his friends. He asked himself: “Who keeps going, no matter how hard it is?” Their perseverance in tough situations reminded him that lousy fortune is universal, not exclusive. Similarly, perceive others around you as your teachers and learn from them.

chapters.title: Come home to yourself
chapters.text:
Have you heard other people preaching to love and accept yourself? If only it were so easy, right? Think of it as running a marathon rather than taking one step. It's a process that takes hundreds, even thousands, of steps to get there.

The willingness to show up changes us. It makes us a little braver each time. ~ Brené Brown

Begin by showing up for yourself. Even small nudges in the right direction can make a big difference in the long run. Start with gratitude by noticing the good things in life, even in small ways. Appreciate your paycheck next time you get it, enjoy a long laugh with your friends, and make it a mission to recognize the beauty and goodness around you. All these moments come together to make your life meaningful.

Self-talk is another aspect of cultivating self-love. Remind yourself you are worthy of protection, firm boundaries, and people who stand up for you. Don't feel ashamed that some parts of you need time to heal. When you're ready, share your vulnerabilities with those you trust.

Setting boundaries and seeking support are acts of self-love.

Self-love also involves embracing all parts of yourself. Some days, you might feel kind and loving; others, you might feel angry or enraged. Both are parts of you, so embrace every part of your fragile, conflicting, complex, yet beautiful self.

As you learn to be gentle with yourself, apply the same approach to others by focusing on love and compassion. When despair or hate creeps in, remind yourself that love is far more powerful. Recognize that love grows as you share it, even when time and resources are scarce. If you find this reminder helpful, write it down and place it somewhere in your room where you'll see it often.

This final meditation can give you the strength to keep going down the better path:
Keep listening to your inner voice. Pay attention and listen. Is it teaching me something? Let me grow in wisdom and peace. Let me root in kindness towards myself and others. Let me accept and give love. Let me trust my voice.

If you're used to self-doubt, trusting your intuition can be challenging. Take inspiration from the monks who focus on quieting the mind. Get very quiet and listen to your inner voice. Over time, this voice will grow stronger, louder, and more trustworthy.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Embrace life's beauty and struggle, and seek out your moments of peace in between. You are not alone in this battle. And yes, you can still find your calm amid the chaos. Become your own safe harbor, protecting yourself against the storms of life that may come your way. Self-love is a long journey that starts with showing up for yourself, setting boundaries, and practicing gratitude for all the good and bad moments you experienced.

Remember that your vulnerability is your strength, even if it comes from pain and heartbreak. Instead of accumulating the hurt, air out your suffering and seek support when needed. And have a beautiful, terrible day!

Try this

• Buy a notebook you enjoy aesthetically and place it on your bedside table. Before sleep, write down things you are thankful for daily.
• Write down at least ten soul-nourishing activities that bring you joy and relaxation. Set aside time each week for at least five of them.

Read it till the end?
